intro.overview: LoneStar DevOps, an Austin-based startup, has an enterprise prospect requiring a third-party security assessment. Their rapid growth has outpaced their security maturity, and with Series B funding on the horizon, they need to know exactly what vulnerabilities exist in their infrastructure. This week, you'll deploy a local simulation of their environment and conduct comprehensive vulnerability discovery using the same methodology NEXUS uses for real client engagements.

mentorQuote.title: Senior Penetration Tester, NEXUS Security
mentorQuote.quote: Scan everything. Version detect everything. The vulnerability you miss is the one that gets exploited. Thoroughness beats speed every time in vulnerability assessment. Amanda Nguyen is counting on us to find what their competitors' security team will find — and we need to find it first.

focusAreas: Docker container deployment and network architecture
Network scanning and service discovery with Nmap
objectives: Deploy the LoneStar DevOps simulated infrastructure using Docker
Conduct comprehensive port scanning and service enumeration
Identify and document 25–30 vulnerabilities across all services
Research CVEs and record base CVSS scores for each finding

rows.task: Checkpoint 1: Deploy LoneStar infrastructure (Docker pull + run)
Checkpoint 4: Application-specific scanning (WPScan, npm audit, MySQL)

totalRow.label: TOTAL
totalRow.note: Scan times vary; plan for Nmap NSE and WPScan to run in the background.

timeGuide.timesheetNote: Scanning time varies based on your system performance. Nmap vulnerability scripts and WPScan can take 10–15 minutes each to complete. Plan for scans to run in the background while you document previous findings.

content.whatYoureDoing: A local simulation of LoneStar DevOps’s infrastructure using Docker containers. This includes their customer portal (WordPress), database server (MySQL), development API (Node.js), and management access (SSH).
content.whyItMatters: Vulnerability assessments require a target environment. Docker allows us to create isolated, reproducible simulations of client infrastructure without touching their production systems. This is how NEXUS tests assessment methodology before deploying to client networks.

steps.text: Verify Docker is running:
Pull the LoneStar infrastructure image from Docker Hub
Deploy all four containers with the provided docker-compose configuration
Verify all containers are running:
Test connectivity to each service (WordPress on 8080, MySQL on 3306, API on 3000, SSH on 2222)

content.code:
# Verify Docker is working
docker --version

# Pull and start all containers
docker-compose -f lonestar-infra.yml up -d

# Verify containers are running
docker ps

# Check container network
docker network inspect bridge

content.description: Capture docker ps output showing all four containers running.
content.body: Take Screenshot 01. Capture the output of docker ps showing all four LoneStar containers running (webapp-prod, db-primary, dev-api, mgmt-ssh). Your VM hostname must be visible in the terminal prompt.

content.description: Capture WordPress landing page at http://localhost:8080.
content.body: Take Screenshot 02. Capture your terminal showing a successful connectivity test to each service — for example, curl output for localhost:8080 (WordPress) and localhost:3000 (API). If using a browser, capture the WordPress login page at localhost:8080 with your VM hostname visible in an adjacent terminal.

items.label: Docker not running
items.text: Start with sudo systemctl start docker

content.text: What is the difference between a vulnerability scanner and a penetration test? Research how automated scanning fits into a security assessment methodology.
You just researched security assessments. Add it to your references: PTES: Penetration Testing Execution Standard. http://www.pentest-standard.org/
Four running Docker containers (webapp-prod, db-primary, dev-api, mgmt-ssh) with accessible services on ports 8080, 3306, 3000, and 2222.
Review Chapter 10 sections on infrastructure security and defense-in-depth. Note how LoneStar’s architecture violates several layered security principles.

content.whatYoureDoing: A complete inventory of LoneStar’s network attack surface — every open port, every running service, and version information for each.
content.whyItMatters: Service enumeration is the foundation of vulnerability assessment. Version detection reveals outdated software with known vulnerabilities. Without accurate version information, you can’t research CVEs or assess risk. This step feeds directly into CVSS scoring in Lab 5.2.

content.title: Junior Security Analyst, NEXUS Security
content.quote: My first vulnerability assessment, I missed an open MySQL port because I only scanned common ports. Check your scans twice. Run a full port scan at least once — you’d be surprised what services companies expose on weird ports.

steps.text: Identify the Docker network range (typically 172.17.0.0/16)
Run a host discovery scan to identify all container IPs
Perform a full port scan on each discovered host
Run service version detection (-sV) on all open ports
Document all services with their exact version numbers

content.code:
# Find Docker network range
ip addr show docker0

# Host discovery on Docker network
sudo nmap -sn 172.17.0.0/24

# Full port scan with version detection
sudo nmap -sV -p- 172.17.0.2-5

# Quick scan of known ports
sudo nmap -sV -sC -p 22,80,3000,3306,8080 172.17.0.2-5

items.description: Host discovery showing all container IPs.

content.body: Take Screenshot 03. Capture the full nmap -sV output from your service version detection scan. The output must show at least one host IP, open ports, and version strings (e.g., Apache 2.4.x, MySQL 5.7.x, OpenSSH). Your VM hostname must be visible in the prompt.

items.label: Wrong subnet
items.text: Scan the Docker bridge network, not your host network. Verify your target range with docker network inspect bridge

items.label: Skipping version detection
items.text: Port numbers alone aren’t enough — always use -sV to identify exact service versions

items.label: Incomplete scan range
items.text: Default Nmap only scans common ports. Use -p- if you suspect services on non-standard ports

content.text: What’s the difference between TCP connect scan (-sT) and SYN scan (-sS)? Check man nmap.
Add to references: Nmap Reference Guide. https://nmap.org/book/man.html
A documented inventory showing: WordPress 5.1.1 on Apache, MySQL 5.7.26, Node.js 14.x API, OpenSSH 7.6p1, plus exact version numbers for all detected services.
Chapter 10 discusses network scanning and enumeration as part of infrastructure assessment. Compare the Nmap output you collected to the scanning methodologies described in the text.

content.whatYoureDoing: An automated vulnerability scan using Nmap’s NSE (Nmap Scripting Engine) to identify known vulnerabilities, misconfigurations, and security weaknesses across all services.
content.whyItMatters: NSE scripts automate vulnerability checking against known CVEs, default credentials, and common misconfigurations. While manual testing is more thorough, automated scans provide baseline coverage and ensure you don’t miss obvious issues.

steps.text: Run the default NSE vulnerability scripts against all containers
Run MySQL-specific scripts against the database container
Run HTTP-specific scripts against the WordPress container
Run SSH-specific scripts against the management container
Document all identified vulnerabilities with CVE numbers where available

content.code:
# Run default vulnerability scripts
sudo nmap --script vuln 172.17.0.2-5

# MySQL vulnerability scanning
sudo nmap --script mysql-vuln* -p 3306 172.17.0.3

# HTTP vulnerability scanning
sudo nmap --script http-vuln* -p 80,8080 172.17.0.2

# SSH-specific scanning
sudo nmap --script ssh* -p 22 172.17.0.5

# Check for default credentials
sudo nmap --script mysql-empty-password -p 3306 172.17.0.3

items.description: NSE vulnerability scan with CVE identified.

content.body: Take Screenshot 04. Capture the NSE vulnerability scan results showing identified findings. The output must include at least one CVE identifier or vulnerability label. If results are long, scroll to show the most significant findings — capture multiple screenshots if needed, labeled 04a, 04b.

items.label: Script category confusion
items.text: --script vuln and --script default run different scripts — know what you’re running

items.label: Scan takes too long
items.text: NSE vuln scripts can be slow. Target specific ports with -p to reduce scan time

items.label: Missing CVE numbers
items.text: Document every CVE ID the scripts report. You’ll need these in Lab 5.2 for CVSS scoring

content.text: What is the CVE system and who maintains it? Research how vulnerabilities are assigned CVE IDs and what CVSS scores represent.
Add: Nmap Scripting Engine (NSE). https://nmap.org/book/nse.html and CVE - MITRE. https://cve.mitre.org/
NSE scan results identifying: MySQL default credentials, Apache CVEs, SSH configuration weaknesses, and at least 5–8 vulnerabilities with CVE numbers documented.
Chapter 10 covers vulnerability scanning as part of infrastructure security assessment. Note how the text distinguishes between authenticated and unauthenticated scans and when each is appropriate.

content.whatYoureDoing: Deep vulnerability analysis using application-specific tools: WPScan for WordPress, npm audit for Node.js, and MySQL client for database security assessment.
content.whyItMatters: Generic scanners miss application-specific vulnerabilities. WPScan knows WordPress vulnerabilities that Nmap doesn’t. npm audit understands Node.js dependency trees. This layered approach ensures comprehensive coverage — it’s how NEXUS achieves thorough assessments.

content.title: Lead DevOps Engineer, LoneStar DevOps
content.quote: I’ve been saying we need to address this for months. Finally, someone’s listening. That npm audit output is going to make Amanda’s eyes pop. We haven’t updated those dependencies since Jake left. Just… be thorough. I need ammunition to get budget for this.

steps.text: Run WPScan against the WordPress installation
Connect to the Node.js container and run npm audit
Test MySQL authentication with default/weak credentials
Check SSH for weak passwords and configuration issues
Document all findings with severity ratings

content.code:
# WordPress vulnerability scan
wpscan --url http://localhost:8080 --enumerate vp,vt,u

# Node.js dependency audit (inside container)
docker exec -it dev-api npm audit

# MySQL credential test
mysql -h localhost -P 3306 -u root -p

# SSH weak password test (try common passwords)
ssh -p 2222 admin@localhost

# Check for former employee account
ssh -p 2222 jdavis@localhost

content.body: Take Screenshot 05. Capture WPScan output showing WordPress vulnerabilities and plugin findings, OR your npm audit output showing Node.js dependency vulnerabilities. The output must show version information and at least one identified vulnerability or finding. Your VM hostname must be visible in the prompt.

items.label: WPScan API token
items.text: Some WPScan features require a free API token. If enumeration returns limited results, check whether you need to register at wpscan.com

items.label: npm audit inside container
items.text: You must docker exec into the container to run npm audit — running it on your host checks your host’s packages, not the target’s

items.label: Tool output overload
items.text: WPScan and npm audit produce verbose output. Focus on high/critical severity findings first and document systematically

content.text: Research CVE-2019-10744 (Lodash prototype pollution). What makes this vulnerability particularly dangerous in Node.js applications? Search the NIST NVD database.

A comprehensive vulnerability inventory with 25–30 findings including: WordPress SQL injection CVE, outdated Apache/PHP, MySQL default credentials, 15+ npm vulnerabilities, weak SSH passwords, and active former employee account (jdavis).

Chapter 20 introduces risk identification and the relationship between vulnerabilities, threats, and assets. The application-specific findings you documented here are exactly the type of data that feeds into the risk assessment process you’ll perform in Lab 5.2.
Take the Lab 5.1 Quiz in Canvas. The quiz verifies you completed the hands-on work — questions will ask about specific outputs you should have seen. Keep your VM running while taking the quiz.

items.label: Attack Surface Mapping
items.text: How to systematically discover every entry point an attacker could exploit.

items.label: Vulnerability Chaining
items.text: Individual findings combine — MySQL access + former employee account = lateral movement.

items.label: Exploitation Path Identification
items.text: Version detection reveals which CVEs apply and which exploits might work.

items.label: Asset Inventory
items.text: You can’t protect what you don’t know exists — accurate service mapping is defensive foundation.

items.label: Patch Prioritization
items.text: CVSS scores and version information drive remediation urgency decisions.

items.label: Account Hygiene
items.text: Former employee accounts (jdavis) represent process failures, not just technical vulnerabilities.

purpleTeam.connection: Vulnerability discovery serves both offense and defense. The same scan that identifies attack vectors also creates the remediation checklist. When red team finds a weak MySQL password, blue team knows exactly what to fix. This is why security assessments help both sides — the report you’ll build isn’t just about finding problems, it’s about giving defenders a roadmap to close the gaps before real attackers find them.

screenshotTable.description: docker ps showing all four containers running
Nmap host discovery with all container IPs identified
Nmap -sV output showing services and exact version numbers
NSE vulnerability scan with at least one CVE identified
npm audit output from inside the dev-api container
SSH finding — weak password or former employee account (jdavis)

items: All four Docker containers running (webapp-prod, db-primary, dev-api, mgmt-ssh)
Host discovery scan completed with all container IPs identified
Service version detection completed for all open ports
NSE vulnerability scripts run against all services
npm audit run inside Node.js container
SSH weak password/former employee testing completed
Vulnerability list documented with CVE numbers (25–30 findings)

finalChecklist.intro: Verify you have completed the following before taking the Lab 5.1 Quiz:

channels.label: Discord
channels.detail: Post in #lab-help for technical issues (fastest response)

needHelp.lateWorkPolicy: Late submissions are not accepted. The course drop policies exist for emergencies — use them wisely. This lab is eligible to be dropped, but don’t use that as an excuse to skip critical assessment skills.

permitted: Explain command syntax and tool functionality
prohibited: Generate fake screenshots or scan outputs
Complete the hands-on scanning work for you
genAI.principle: Think of AI like a tutor, not a ghostwriter. It can help you understand and improve, but the work itself must be yours.

whatsNext.title: Lab 5.2: Risk Assessment & Prioritization
whatsNext.text: Next week you’ll score each vulnerability using CVSS, assess business impact, and build a prioritization framework. LoneStar can’t fix everything before their enterprise review — which vulnerabilities matter most?
